era_context: The Classical Greek world in the mid-5th century BCE, during the period of Athenian expansion and consolidation of the Delian League. Cimon is at the height of his power and influence in Athens, leading successful military campaigns. Sparta remains the dominant land power but is not yet in direct conflict with Athens. The Persian Empire under Artaxerxes I is stable but facing periodic rebellions in its peripheral territories. This is a period of relative stability before the major conflicts that would define the later 5th century.

events.title: Athenian victory at the Battle of Eurymedon
events.description: Cimon led an Athenian naval and land force to victory against a combined Persian fleet and army at the Eurymedon River in Pamphylia. This battle is considered one of the most significant Athenian victories against the Persians and marked the effective end of major Persian naval operations in the eastern Mediterranean. The victory greatly enhanced Athenian prestige and power, solidifying Cimon's position as the leading figure in Athens. However, the precise dating of this battle is one of the most disputed questions in Classical chronology, with scholars proposing dates ranging from 469 to 465 BCE.

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War, Book I

events.certainty_note: The Battle of Eurymedon is well-attested in ancient sources, but its dating is highly uncertain. Ancient sources do not provide a precise date, and modern scholars have proposed dates ranging from 469 to 465 BCE. The date of 467 BCE is one of several plausible reconstructions, but it is not definitively established. Some scholars favor 466 BCE, others 469 BCE.
cross_references: Part of broader Athenian expansion against Persia
Led to increased Athenian dominance in the Aegean

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of the Battle of Eurymedon is one of the most contentious issues in Classical Greek chronology. While traditionally placed in the 460s BCE, modern scholarship is deeply divided. Some scholars argue for 469 BCE, others for 466 BCE, and still others for 465 BCE. The ancient sources themselves do not provide a precise date, and the reconstruction depends on synchronization with other chronological systems (particularly the Athenian archon list) that are themselves uncertain. The significance of the battle is undisputed, but its precise historical placement remains highly problematic. Some scholars have questioned whether the battle occurred as described in the sources, though this view is minority opinion.
historiographic_note: The year 467 BCE is poorly documented in surviving sources. The only event of potential significance is the Battle of Eurymedon, but even this is not securely dated to 467 BCE. The historical record for this year is dominated by Greek sources, particularly Thucydides and Plutarch, both writing long after the events. There is virtually no documentation from Persian, Egyptian, or other non-Greek perspectives. The dating of events in this period is highly uncertain and depends on the reconstruction of the Athenian archon list, which itself contains significant gaps and uncertainties. The Western bias toward Greek history is pronounced, and the absence of documentation from other regions means that major events in Asia, Africa, and elsewhere are likely completely unrecorded in surviving sources.

graph_edges.note: The Battle of Eurymedon may have occurred in 467, 466, or 465 BCE; the dating is uncertain
